title: Chapter 3 _Data Modeling Using the Entity-Relationship (ER) Model

questions.text: What are the two main activities involved in the database design process as described in the text?
questions.explanation: According to Slide 4, the database design process involves two main activities: Database design (conceptual schema) and Applications design (programs and interfaces).

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Entities' in the ER Model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Properties used to describe an object

choices.label: B
choices.text: Specific objects or things in the mini-world represented in the database

choices.label: D
choices.text: The data types associated with attributes

questions.explanation: Entities are specific objects or things in the mini-world that are represented in the database, such as a specific employee or department (Slide 8).

questions.text: An attribute that can be divided into smaller sub-parts with independent meanings (e.g., Name into FirstName, MiddleName, LastName) is called a:
questions.explanation: A composite attribute may be composed of several components, such as Address or Name (Slide 9).

questions.text: How is a multi-valued attribute represented in an ER diagram?
questions.explanation: In ER diagrams, multi-valued attributes are displayed in double ovals (Slide 14).

questions.text:
Which notation is used to represent an Entity Type in an ER diagram?

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Rectangular Box] 
    B((Oval))
    C{Diamond}
    D[(Cylinder)]
```
questions.explanation: Entity types are displayed in rectangular boxes (Slide 14).

questions.text: What is a 'Key Attribute' of an entity type?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An attribute that must be a string

choices.label: B
choices.text: An attribute that can have null values

choices.label: C
choices.text: An attribute for which each entity must have a unique value

choices.label: D
choices.text: An attribute that links two entities

questions.explanation: A key attribute is an attribute for which each entity must have a unique value, such as SSN for EMPLOYEE (Slide 12).

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding a 'Weak Entity Type'?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It does not have a key attribute of its own

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is represented by a single rectangle

questions.explanation: A weak entity type is an entity that does not have a key attribute and must participate in an identifying relationship (Slide 29).

questions.text: In the COMPANY database example, if an EMPLOYEE is identified by SSN and a DEPENDENT is identified by the combination of the EMPLOYEE's SSN and the DEPENDENT's Name, the Name of the DEPENDENT is called a:
questions.explanation: The Name of a DEPENDENT is a partial key because it identifies the weak entity only in combination with the owner entity (Slide 29).

questions.text: What does the 'Degree' of a relationship type represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of attributes in the relationship

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of participating entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: The maximum number of entities allowed

choices.label: D
choices.text: The level of nesting in attributes

questions.explanation: The degree of a relationship type is the number of participating entity types (Slide 20).

questions.text: A relationship type where the same entity type participates more than once in different roles is called a:
questions.explanation: A recursive relationship type is one in which the same participating entity type exists in distinct roles (Slide 28).

questions.text: In a 1:N relationship (e.g., WORKS_FOR between DEPARTMENT and EMPLOYEE), where is the relationship attribute usually best placed if it exists?

choices.label: D
choices.text: It must stay on the relationship diamond

questions.explanation: In 1:N relationships, relationship attributes can be transferred to the entity type on the N-side (Slide 36).

questions.text: What is the meaning of a 'Total Participation' constraint (existence dependency)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every entity in the entity set must participate in at least one relationship instance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Entities can optionally participate in the relationship

choices.label: C
choices.text: Each entity can only participate in one relationship instance

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relationship must have at least two attributes

questions.explanation: Total participation (existence dependency) means every entity in the set must participate in the relationship, shown by a double line (Slide 30, 38).

questions.text: Using (min, max) notation, if an employee can manage at most one department but may manage none, the participation of EMPLOYEE in the MANAGES relationship is:
questions.explanation: Min=0 means optional participation; Max=1 means at most one relationship instance (Slide 39).

questions.text: How does UML represent entity types (classes)?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Large rounded boxes with three sections

questions.explanation: UML class diagrams represent classes as large rounded boxes with three sections: name, attributes, and operations (Slide 44).

questions.text:
In an ER diagram, how is a relationship type visualized?

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Legend
    R1[Rect] --- Entity
    R2{Diamond} --- Relationship
    R3((Oval)) --- Attribute
    end
```
questions.explanation: A diamond-shaped box is used to display a relationship type (Slide 24).

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a multi-valued attribute from the provided text?
questions.explanation: According to Slide 6 and Slide 9, a department may have several locations, making it a multi-valued attribute.

questions.text: What is the difference between a 'Relationship Type' and a 'Relationship Set'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A type is the current state; a set is the schema description

choices.label: B
choices.text: A type is the schema description; a set is the current collection of instances

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are identical terms and used interchangeably

choices.label: D
choices.text: A type refers to binary relationships; a set refers to ternary relationships

questions.explanation: Relationship Type is the schema description; Relationship Set is the current set of instances in the database (Slide 23).

questions.text: If a relationship involves three entity types, it is called a:
questions.explanation: Relationship types of degree 3 are called ternary (Slide 46).

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Role Names' in an ER diagram?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To name the primary key of an entity

choices.label: B
choices.text: To distinguish the different participations of an entity type in a recursive relationship

choices.label: C
choices.text: To identify the data type of an attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: To list the operations in a UML class

questions.explanation: Role names are used to distinguish participations, especially in recursive relationships like SUPERVISION (Slide 33).

questions.text: A 'VehicleTagNumber' is a key of the CAR entity type with components (Number, State). This is an example of a:
questions.explanation: A key attribute may be composite, such as VehicleTagNumber which consists of (Number, State) (Slide 13).

questions.text: In (min, max) notation, what does a `min = 0` signify?
questions.explanation: Min=0 signifies optional participation (Slide 39).

questions.text: Which attribute type is denoted by curly braces { } in text descriptions (e.g., {Color})?
questions.explanation: Multi-valued attributes are denoted as {Color} or {PreviousDegrees} (Slide 9).

questions.text: The 'WORKS_ON' relationship in the COMPANY database between EMPLOYEE and PROJECT is likely to be of which cardinality ratio, given an employee can work on several projects and a project can have several employees?
questions.explanation: Because an employee works on several projects and a project involves several employees, it is a many-to-many (M:N) relationship (Slide 22, 30).

questions.text: What does a double-lined rectangle represent in an ER diagram?
questions.explanation: While Slide 14 mentions rectangles for entity types, standard ER notation (and common usage for identifying weak entities) uses double rectangles for weak entities. Slide 43's summary confirms double rectangles for weak entities.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT part of the basic ER model concepts but is included in UML class diagrams?
questions.explanation: Slide 44 states that operations are not in the basic ER model but are included in the third section of UML class diagrams.

questions.text: Identify the degree of the SUPERVISION relationship.
questions.explanation: SUPERVISION is a binary relationship (degree 2) because it relates two entities of the same type (Slide 25).

questions.text: If a relationship attribute 'Hours' is associated with the 'WORKS_ON' relationship between EMPLOYEE and PROJECT, it means:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total hours worked by an employee across all projects

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total hours spent on a project by all employees

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of hours a specific employee works on a specific project

questions.explanation: The value of a relationship attribute depends on the particular combination of participating entities (Slide 36).

questions.text: A 'Value Set' associated with an attribute is essentially its:
questions.explanation: Each attribute has a value set (or data type) associated with it, such as integer or string (Slide 8).

questions.text: How is a 'Total Participation' constraint represented in an ER diagram notation?
questions.explanation: Total participation is shown by a double line (Slide 38).

questions.text: In the (min, max) notation, where is the constraint displayed?

choices.label: B
choices.text: On the oval of the attribute

choices.label: C
choices.text: On the edge connecting the entity type and the relationship type

questions.explanation: The (min, max) notation is specified on each participation of an entity type E in a relationship type R, effectively on the connecting edges (Slide 39).

questions.text: If a ternary relationship exists between SUPPLIER, PART, and PROJECT, which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can always be replaced by three binary relationships without loss of information

choices.label: B
choices.text: It represents an association between three entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: It must have at least one weak entity

questions.explanation: A ternary relationship involves three entity types and is generally not equivalent to three binary relationships (Slide 46, 47).

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a 'negative' of many popular data modeling tools?

choices.label: C
choices.text: They often lack proper notation for relationship attributes

questions.explanation: Most tools lack a proper distinct notation for relationships with relationship attributes (Slide 52).

questions.text: In the COMPANY database, the 'DEPENDENTS_OF' relationship is an example of an:
questions.explanation: A weak entity must participate in an identifying relationship type with an owner entity type; DEPENDENT participates in DEPENDENTS_OF (Slide 29).

questions.text: If an attribute `PreviousDegrees` is defined as `{PreviousDegrees (College, Year, Degree, Field)}`, it is a:
questions.explanation: It is multi-valued (multiple degrees possible) and composite (each degree has sub-components) (Slide 10).

questions.text: What is an 'Entity Set'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The collection of all entity types in the schema

choices.label: B
choices.text: A collection of entities of the same type currently stored in the database

choices.label: C
choices.text: The list of attributes for an entity

choices.label: D
choices.text: A set of keys for a specific entity

questions.explanation: An entity set is the collection of all entities of a particular entity type at any point in time (Slide 16).

questions.text: Which of these is a valid constraint on a (min, max) specification?
questions.explanation: According to Slide 39, we must have min ≤ max, min ≥ 0, and max ≥ 1.

questions.text: In ER Diagrams, how are components of a composite attribute connected?

choices.label: B
choices.text: To the oval representing the composite attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using double lines to the entity

questions.explanation: Components of a composite attribute are connected to the oval representing the composite attribute (Slide 14).

questions.text: What does a '1' on an edge of a higher-degree relationship indicate?

choices.label: C
choices.text: That an entity can participate in at most one relationship instance given a unique combination of other entities

choices.label: D
choices.text: That there is only one entity type in the relationship

questions.explanation: In higher-degree relationships, a 1 indicates that an entity can participate in at most one relationship instance for a particular combination of the other participating entities (Slide 51).

questions.text: Which notation is typically used to represent a partial key?
questions.explanation: In ER diagrams, key attributes are underlined. Partial keys are typically represented with a dashed underline (implied by standard ER notation in Slide 43's summary table).

questions.text: In the (min, max) notation, what is the default constraint if none is specified?
questions.explanation: Default (no constraint) is min=0, max=n, signifying no limit (Slide 39).

questions.text: Relationships of the same type are grouped into a:
questions.explanation: Relationships of the same type are grouped or typed into a relationship type (Slide 20).

questions.text: Which model extension is mentioned to support specialization and generalization abstractions?
questions.explanation: The Extended Entity-Relationship (EER) model supports specialization and generalization (Slide 53).

questions.text: In UML class diagrams, relationships are called:
questions.explanation: In UML, relationships are represented as lines connecting classes and are called associations (Slide 44).

questions.text: A specific employee entity has the attribute Name='John Smith'. Here, 'John Smith' is a/an:
questions.explanation: A specific entity will have a value for each of its attributes (Slide 8).

questions.text: In the COMPANY database, the 'SUPERVISION' relationship involves which entity type?
questions.explanation: The SUPERVISION relationship is recursive, where an EMPLOYEE participates twice (as supervisor and supervisee) (Slide 28).

questions.text: What is the primary focus of the database design process as discussed in the context of conceptual schema design?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Designing the conceptual schema for a database application

questions.explanation: Database design focus is to design the conceptual schema, while application design focuses on programs and interfaces.

questions.text: Which of the following is defined as a specific object or thing in the mini-world that is represented in the database?
questions.explanation: Entities are specific objects or things in the mini-world, like a specific employee or project.

questions.text: An attribute that can be divided into smaller sub-parts is known as a:
questions.explanation: Composite attributes may be composed of several components, such as an Address consisting of City, State, and Zip.

questions.text: In ER diagrams, how are multi-valued attributes represented?
questions.explanation: Multivalued attributes are displayed in double ovals in ER diagrams.

questions.text: What does a 'Key Attribute' represent in an entity type?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An attribute that must have a null value

choices.label: B
choices.text: An attribute for which each entity must have a unique value

choices.label: C
choices.text: An attribute that is shared by all entities

choices.label: D
choices.text: A multi-valued attribute used for indexing

questions.explanation: An attribute of an entity type for which each entity must have a unique value is called a key attribute.

questions.text: Which attribute of a STUDENT entity would be considered multi-valued if they have several previous degrees?
questions.explanation: PreviousDegrees is a multi-valued attribute because an entity can have multiple values for it.

questions.text: A 'Relationship Type' is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The current state of relationship instances

choices.label: B
choices.text: The schema description of a relationship

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of entities in a database

choices.label: D
choices.text: A collection of attributes for an entity

questions.explanation: Relationship Type is the schema description that identifies the name and participating entity types.

questions.text: What is the 'degree' of a relationship type?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of attributes in the relationship

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of participating entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: The maximum cardinality of the relationship

choices.label: D
choices.text: The total number of relationship instances

questions.explanation: The degree of a relationship type is the number of participating entity types (e.g., binary, ternary).

questions.text: In the COMPANY database example, what type of relationship is 'WORKS_FOR' between EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT?
questions.explanation: WORKS_FOR is an N:1 relationship because many employees work for one department.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a 'Weak Entity Type'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An entity that has multiple keys

choices.label: B
choices.text: An entity that does not have a key attribute

choices.label: C
choices.text: An entity with only one attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: An entity that cannot participate in relationships

questions.explanation: A weak entity type is an entity that does not have a key attribute of its own.

questions.text: How is a weak entity identified?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By its own unique primary key

choices.label: B
choices.text: By a combination of its partial key and the key of its identifying owner entity

questions.explanation: Weak entities are identified by the combination of a partial key and the specific entity they are related to in the identifying entity type.

questions.text: In an ER diagram, what symbol is used to represent a relationship type?
questions.explanation: A diamond-shaped box is used to display a relationship type in ER diagrams.

questions.text: Which constraint specifies the maximum number of relationship instances an entity can participate in?
questions.explanation: Cardinality ratio specifies the maximum participation (1:1, 1:N, M:N).

questions.text: A 'Recursive Relationship' occurs when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Three different entities participate in the relationship

choices.label: B
choices.text: An entity type participates more than once in the same relationship type in different roles

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relationship connects a weak entity to its owner

questions.explanation: A recursive relationship type is one where the same entity type participates in distinct roles.

questions.text: Total participation in a relationship is also known as:
questions.explanation: Total participation (mandatory) is also called existence dependency.

questions.text: How is total participation represented in an ER diagram?
questions.explanation: Total participation is shown by a double line connecting the entity type to the relationship.

questions.text: Attributes can be assigned to relationship types. This is most common in which type of relationship ratio?
questions.explanation: Most relationship attributes are used with M:N relationships, as the value depends on the combination of both entities.

questions.text: In the (min, max) notation, if the participation of an entity is optional, the 'min' value is:
questions.explanation: A min value of 0 signifies optional participation (no constraint).

questions.text: If a department must have exactly one manager and an employee can manage at most one department, what is the (min, max) notation for EMPLOYEE in the MANAGES relationship?
questions.explanation: Since an employee can manage 'at most one' (0 or 1), the notation is (0, 1).

questions.text: What is the degree of a 'ternary' relationship?
questions.explanation: Relationship types of degree 3 are called ternary.

questions.text: In UML class diagrams, what corresponds to an 'entity type' in an ER diagram?
questions.explanation: UML class diagrams represent classes, which are similar to entity types.

questions.text: In UML class diagrams, the second section of the class box includes:
questions.explanation: The top section includes the name, the second includes attributes, and the third includes operations.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding n-ary relationships?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An n-ary relationship is always equivalent to n binary relationships.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constraints are easier to specify for n-ary relationships than for binary relationships.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An n-ary relationship is not necessarily equivalent to n binary relationships.

questions.explanation: In general, an n-ary relationship is not equivalent to n binary relationships and constraints are harder to specify.

questions.text: If a binary relationship can be derived from a higher-degree relationship at all times, it is considered:
questions.explanation: If a particular binary relationship can be derived from a higher-degree relationship, then it is redundant.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an example of a database modeling tool mentioned in the text?
questions.explanation: ERWin, S-Designer, and ER-Studio are listed as popular tools.

questions.text: What is the 'partial key' of the DEPENDENT weak entity type in the COMPANY example?
questions.explanation: The name of the DEPENDENT is the partial key that identifies it in conjunction with an EMPLOYEE.

questions.text: Consider the attribute Address(House#, Street, City, State). This is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Address is composite as it is made of sub-components like House#, Street, etc.

questions.text: The collection of all entities of a particular entity type in the database at a specific point in time is called the:
questions.explanation: Each entity type will have a collection of entities stored in the database called the entity set.

questions.text: A specific entity instance for EMPLOYEE might be Name='John Smith', SSN='123456789'. This '123456789' value belongs to the attribute's:
questions.explanation: Each attribute has a value set (or data type) associated with it.

questions.text: Which relationship type relates an EMPLOYEE to their supervisor in the COMPANY database?
questions.explanation: The SUPERVISION relationship tracks the direct supervisor of each employee.

questions.text: In a recursive relationship, why are role names used in the ER diagram?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To identify the value set of the attribute

choices.label: B
choices.text: To distinguish between the different participations of the same entity type

choices.label: D
choices.text: To represent the weak entity owner

questions.explanation: In recursive relationships, role names distinguish participations (e.g., supervisor vs. supervisee).

questions.text: If an attribute is denoted as \( \{Color\} \), what does this notation imply?
questions.explanation: Multi-valued attributes are often denoted with curly braces, e.g., {Color}.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a nested attribute structure based on the text?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A simple attribute with multiple values

choices.label: B
choices.text: A composite attribute whose components are themselves composite or multi-valued

choices.label: C
choices.text: A key attribute that is also a foreign key

choices.label: D
choices.text: An entity that has no attributes

questions.explanation: Composite and multi-valued attributes may be nested arbitrarily, such as PreviousDegrees having sub-components.

questions.text: In an ER diagram, if a line connecting an entity to a relationship is a single line, it signifies:
questions.explanation: Partial participation is shown by a single line in ER diagrams.

questions.text: A department may have several locations. In the ER diagram, 'Locations' for DEPARTMENT would be represented as:
questions.explanation: Since a department can have 'several' locations, it is a multi-valued attribute, represented by a double oval.

questions.text: The relationship 'CONTROLS' exists between which two entity types?
questions.explanation: According to the requirements, each department controls a number of projects.

questions.text: When using (min, max) notation, what does (1, n) signify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Optional participation with a maximum of 1

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mandatory participation with no upper limit

choices.label: C
choices.text: Optional participation with no upper limit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mandatory participation with a maximum of 1

questions.explanation: Min=1 means mandatory (at least one) and max=n means no limit (many).

questions.text: Which component of the ER model was NOT supported in its original form and requires the 'Extended ER (EER)' model?
questions.explanation: The original ER model did not support specialization and generalization; these are part of the EER model.

questions.text: In the COMPANY database, an employee works for 'one' department, and a department can have 'any number' of employees. What is the (min, max) for EMPLOYEE in the WORKS_FOR relationship?
questions.explanation: Since an employee works for exactly one department, the constraint is (1, 1).

questions.text: A 'Relationship Set' is defined as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The list of attributes of a relationship

choices.label: B
choices.text: The current state/collection of relationship instances in the database

choices.label: C
choices.text: The structural constraints of a relationship

choices.label: D
choices.text: The primary key of the relationship

questions.explanation: A relationship set is the current state of a relationship type, containing the current instances represented in the database.

questions.text: In UML, what are relationships between classes called?
questions.explanation: In UML class diagrams, relationships are called associations.

questions.text: According to the slide on modeling tools, what is a common 'negative' of these tools?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They mostly represent relational design rather than true conceptual ER design.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are only compatible with Oracle databases.

questions.explanation: A negative is that most tools represent a relational design in diagrammatic form rather than a conceptual ER-based design.

questions.text: The 'HoursPerWeek' attribute in the COMPANY database belongs to which relationship?
questions.explanation: HoursPerWeek is an attribute of the WORKS_ON relationship, depending on the (employee, project) combination.

questions.text: What is the key attribute for the CAR entity type if it is identified by Number and State?
questions.explanation: VehicleTagNumber is a key of CAR with components (Number, State), making it a composite key.

questions.text: In a 1:N relationship, where can relationship attributes be transferred?
questions.explanation: In 1:N relationships, relationship attributes can be transferred to the entity type on the N-side.
